subject: EU Law
lesson_title: Limits and Retrenchment: Ruiz Zambrano, McCarthy, and Dano

questions.question: Ruiz Zambrano v Office national de l'emploi [2011] (C-34/09) is a landmark EU citizenship case. Mr Ruiz Zambrano (Colombian) and his wife had children born in Belgium who were Belgian nationals (and thus EU citizens). Mr Ruiz Zambrano applied for the right to work and reside in Belgium. The CJEU held:

options.A: That Mr Ruiz Zambrano had no EU law rights as a third-country national whose children had never exercised free movement
options.B: That Article 20 TFEU precludes national measures that would deprive EU citizen children of the genuine enjoyment of the substance of the rights attached to their EU citizenship status — refusing the father the right to reside and work in Belgium would compel the EU citizen children to leave the EU territory, which would deprive them of their EU citizenship rights
options.C: That Belgian citizenship conferred on children of undocumented migrants is contrary to EU law
options.D: That parents of EU citizen children automatically acquire EU citizenship themselves

questions.explanation: Ruiz Zambrano created a new constitutional principle — the 'genuine enjoyment of the substance of EU citizenship rights' test. The CJEU held that Article 20 TFEU precludes national measures that have the effect of depriving EU citizens (particularly children) of the genuine enjoyment of the substance of the rights attached to their EU citizenship status. Refusing the father the right to work and reside would effectively compel the Belgian children to leave the EU to follow their parents — stripping them of their citizenship rights. This holding applies even where the EU citizen child has never moved between member states (a purely internal situation) — the threat to citizenship substance itself triggers EU law protection. This was an extraordinary extension of EU citizenship.

questions.question: McCarthy v Secretary of State for the Home Department [2011] (C-434/09) distinguished Ruiz Zambrano. Ms McCarthy was a British national who also held Irish nationality. She married a Jamaican national and claimed a residence card for him based on her EU citizenship. The CJEU held:

options.A: That Ms McCarthy's Jamaican husband had an automatic right of residence as a family member of an EU citizen
options.B: That EU free movement law did not apply — Ms McCarthy had never exercised free movement rights (she had always resided in the UK); the Directive 2004/38 applied only to EU citizens who moved to another member state; there was no measure depriving her of the 'substance' of her EU citizenship rights since she was not being compelled to leave the EU
options.C: That dual EU national citizens always have full free movement rights wherever they reside
options.D: That the UK could not deny residence rights to the spouse of a dual national

questions.explanation: McCarthy distinguished Ruiz Zambrano on its facts: Ms McCarthy had never exercised free movement — she had always lived in the UK. Directive 2004/38 applies only to EU citizens who move to a member state other than their own. As she had not moved, the Directive did not grant her Jamaican husband a residence right. The CJEU also found the Ruiz Zambrano 'substance' principle was inapplicable: Ms McCarthy was not being compelled to leave the EU — she could remain in the UK. The fact that the UK was simultaneously her member state of nationality and residence meant that purely national immigration rules applied. McCarthy confirmed that EU citizenship does not create free movement rights in purely internal situations unless the 'substance' test applies.

questions.question: Dereci v Bundesministerium für Inneres [2011] (C-256/11) applied the Ruiz Zambrano principle in the context of third-country national family members of Austrian citizens who had never exercised free movement. The CJEU clarified the 'substance' test:

options.A: That the mere fact that it would be desirable for family unity reasons for a third-country national family member to stay in the EU is sufficient to engage Article 20 TFEU
options.B: That the 'substance' test requires that the EU citizen would effectively be compelled to leave the EU territory as a whole — economic or social desirability of the family member's presence is not sufficient; only if the EU citizen would actually have to leave the EU does the substance of citizenship rights become engaged
options.C: That third-country national spouses of EU citizens always have a right to reside in the EU under Article 20 TFEU
options.D: That Article 20 TFEU applies whenever an EU citizen would experience significant hardship from expulsion of a family member

questions.explanation: Dereci clarified that the Ruiz Zambrano principle has a narrow scope. The CJEU held that the test is whether the EU citizen is compelled — not merely inconvenienced or disadvantaged — to leave EU territory. The fact that a third-country national parent, spouse, or other family member contributes to the EU citizen's wellbeing, finances, or emotional support is insufficient to engage Article 20 — family unity and quality of life, while important, are protected under national or ECHR law, not Article 20 TFEU. Article 20 applies only where effective deprivation of citizenship substance occurs (as in Ruiz Zambrano, where young children had no real choice but to follow their parents). Dereci significantly limited the potential sweep of Ruiz Zambrano.

questions.question: Dano v Jobcenter Leipzig [2014] (C-333/13) involved a Romanian national living in Germany who had never worked and applied for basic subsistence benefits. The CJEU held:

options.A: That EU citizenship requires Germany to provide all EU citizens with basic subsistence regardless of economic activity
options.B: That Article 24(2) of Directive 2004/38 permits member states to refuse social assistance to economically inactive EU citizens who do not satisfy the Article 7(1)(b) conditions (sufficient resources + comprehensive health insurance) — Ms Dano did not satisfy these conditions and Germany was entitled to refuse benefits without conducting an individual assessment of her circumstances
options.C: That Ms Dano was a worker because she had previously worked briefly in Romania
options.D: That Germany had to provide a minimum level of subsistence to all EU citizens regardless of compliance with Directive 2004/38

questions.explanation: Dano marks the most significant retrenchment in citizenship social rights. The CJEU held clearly that an EU citizen who moves to another member state without satisfying Article 7(1)(b) conditions (sufficient resources and comprehensive health insurance) cannot claim equal treatment in social assistance — Article 24(2) expressly permits exclusion in such cases. Crucially, no individualised proportionality assessment is required before refusal — the Directive itself provides the answer. Ms Dano had arrived in Germany specifically without sufficient resources and had not worked; she could not benefit from EU citizenship's equal treatment provisions to claim German basic subsistence benefits. Dano firmly re-established that EU citizenship does not create unconditional social rights for non-economically-active EU citizens.

questions.question: Alimanovic [2015] (C-67/14) built on Dano and addressed Swedish nationals who had worked briefly in Germany and then ceased working. They claimed German social assistance (basic livelihood benefits). The CJEU held:

options.A: That all former workers are entitled to social assistance indefinitely under Article 7(3) of the Directive
options.B: That after six months of unemployment (beyond the period of worker status retention under Article 7(3)(c)), the claimants no longer satisfied the Article 7 residence conditions and Germany could refuse social assistance without conducting an individual assessment — the Directive itself provided the balance of interests; no case-by-case proportionality was required
options.C: That Sweden was responsible for providing social assistance to its nationals wherever they resided in the EU
options.D: That job-seekers retain full worker status and social assistance rights indefinitely while genuinely seeking work

questions.explanation: Alimanovic confirmed and extended Dano: once the period of retained worker status under Article 7(3) ends (after six months of involuntary unemployment for those who worked for less than one year), the claimants no longer satisfy the Article 7 residence conditions and can be excluded from social assistance without individualised assessment. The Court held that the Directive itself already reflects a careful balance between the interests of the claimant and the host member state — no further proportionality analysis is needed. The legislative balance struck in the Directive is presumed proportionate for these categories of persons. Alimanovic definitively closed the 'Grzelczyk door' to social assistance for those who fail to meet the Directive's conditions.

questions.question: García-Nieto [2016] (C-299/14) concerned Spanish nationals in their first three months in Germany seeking basic subsistence benefits. The CJEU held:

options.A: That all EU citizens in the host state for any period are entitled to social assistance
options.B: That during the first three months of residence, Article 24(2) of Directive 2004/38 permits the host state to exclude all EU citizens (other than workers) from social assistance entirely — this is compatible with EU law and requires no individual assessment
options.C: That three months is too short a period to justify exclusion from social assistance
options.D: That EU citizens may be excluded from social assistance only after a court order

questions.explanation: García-Nieto confirmed the layered approach to social assistance and EU citizenship. During the first three months of residence (Article 6 right), Article 24(2) expressly permits member states to refuse social assistance to all EU citizens who are not workers or self-employed. No individual assessment is required — the blanket exclusion for the first three months is authorised by the Directive and compatible with EU law. The CJEU noted that for this very short initial period, the Directive has already made the policy choice: EU citizens in the first three months must support themselves. This prevents social tourism by those arriving in the host state specifically to claim benefits without any prior integration.

questions.question: The concept of 'social tourism' (or 'benefit tourism') has been discussed in the context of EU citizenship cases. What is the legal significance of this concept in EU law?

options.A: Social tourism is prohibited by an express provision of the TFEU
options.B: While 'social tourism' is not a legal category in EU law, the CJEU has recognised that member states have a legitimate interest in preventing EU citizens from moving specifically to claim social benefits — this justifies conditions (sufficient resources, genuine link) on social entitlements; the concept has influenced the restrictive approach in Dano, Alimanovic, and García-Nieto
options.C: Social tourism is defined in Directive 2004/38 as a ground for automatic expulsion
options.D: The concept has no legal significance — all EU citizens have unconditional rights to social benefits in any member state

questions.explanation: Social tourism (moving to claim benefits, sometimes called 'benefit tourism') is not a formal legal concept in EU law — but it has political and judicial significance. The CJEU in Dano and subsequent cases acknowledged the member state interest in preventing movement motivated primarily by access to better social benefit systems. This underpins the conditions in Directive 2004/38 (sufficient resources, genuine link) and the Dano/Alimanovic line of authority permitting blanket exclusions without individual assessment. The concept influenced the Court's willingness to uphold restrictive national rules and reject the Grzelczyk era's implied proportionality safety net. However, EU law still prohibits stereotyping all EU citizens as benefit tourists — the conditions must be assessed individually where required.

questions.question: Pisciotti v Bundesrepublik Deutschland [2018] (C-191/16) concerned extradition of an Italian national from Germany to the US under a bilateral EU-US extradition agreement. The CJEU addressed whether EU citizenship rights were engaged. The Court held:

options.A: That EU citizenship prevents extradition of any EU citizen to third countries
options.B: That where a member state acts within the scope of EU law (implementing an EU-US extradition agreement), it must comply with the principle of non-discrimination on grounds of nationality — Germany could extradite Mr Pisciotti only if it treated EU nationals from other member states in the same way as German nationals (who could not be extradited under the German Constitution)
options.C: That extradition is a matter of national criminal law entirely outside EU law
options.D: That EU citizenship provides immunity from extradition to non-EU countries

questions.explanation: Pisciotti illustrates the scope of EU citizenship in criminal and extradition contexts. When a member state acts within the scope of EU law (implementing an EU agreement), it must respect the non-discrimination principle. Germany's EU-US extradition agreement was an EU-law measure — by implementing it, Germany was acting within EU law's scope. Therefore, the principle of non-discrimination on grounds of nationality (Article 18 TFEU) applied: Germany could not extradite Italian national Pisciotti when it would not extradite German nationals in similar circumstances. However, the Court left to the national court to assess the proportionality and available remedies. This case shows that EU citizenship protection can arise in unexpected contexts whenever member states act within EU law's scope.

questions.question: What is the significance of the 'scope of EU law' requirement for Article 18 TFEU (non-discrimination on grounds of nationality) to apply?

options.A: Article 18 applies universally — any discrimination on nationality grounds anywhere is prohibited
options.B: Article 18 TFEU prohibits discrimination on grounds of nationality only 'within the scope of application of the Treaties' — it is not a freestanding prohibition; the situation must have a genuine connection to EU law (e.g., exercise of free movement, implementation of EU legislation, application of EU fundamental rights) before Article 18 is engaged
options.C: Article 18 applies only to economic discrimination — social discrimination is outside its scope
options.D: Article 18 applies only when expressly invoked alongside another Treaty article

questions.explanation: The scope limitation in Article 18 TFEU is critical and frequently litigated. Article 18 prohibits nationality discrimination only within the scope of the Treaties — it is not a general universal equality principle. For Article 18 to apply: the situation must fall within a field regulated by EU law; or the individual must have exercised EU free movement rights; or the situation must be connected to EU law in some other relevant way. Purely internal situations with no EU law connection are outside Article 18's scope. This is why Ruiz Zambrano was exceptional — the CJEU found a connection to EU citizenship directly (Article 20 TFEU) even without movement, through the substance of citizenship. Without such a connection, Article 18 and Article 21 do not apply.

questions.question: Following Dano, Alimanovic, and García-Nieto, what remains of the Grzelczyk principle that EU citizenship is the 'fundamental status' of member state nationals?

options.A: Nothing — Dano and subsequent cases have overruled Grzelczyk entirely
options.B: The 'fundamental status' principle remains alive: EU citizenship continues to confer substantive rights of equal treatment and residence; but it does not override the Directive's explicit conditions — the social rights flowing from citizenship are proportionate to integration; workers and permanent residents receive fuller equal treatment; economically inactive citizens who satisfy Article 7 conditions are protected; those who do not satisfy conditions may be excluded from social assistance
options.C: The fundamental status principle now applies only to political rights (voting, petitioning)
options.D: The fundamental status principle applies only to non-discrimination in employment

questions.explanation: The post-Dano synthesis is that EU citizenship remains a fundamental status but one whose social rights are calibrated to the degree of integration. The Directive creates a graduated system: (1) workers and permanent residents have the strongest social rights (effectively full equal treatment); (2) economically inactive citizens satisfying Article 7 conditions have full equal treatment except for the Article 24(2) exclusions; (3) those not satisfying Article 7 conditions may be excluded from social assistance. The 'fundamental status' principle still protects against discriminatory obstacles to movement, ensures non-discrimination in political rights, and operationalises the Ruiz Zambrano substance principle. It has not been overruled — its social implications have simply been bounded by the Directive's own graduated framework.
